title: Unemployment
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS unemployment — types, measurement, AD–AS analysis, and policy responses.
learningObjectives: Explain how unemployment is measured (claimant count, ILO/Labour Force Survey).
Use AD–AS to show demand-deficient unemployment.

simpleExplanation.title: When labour is unused
simpleExplanation.summary: Unemployment means willing and able workers cannot find jobs. Cyclical unemployment links to deficient AD; structural to skills mismatch.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Frictional unemployment is like changing buses — briefly between rides. Structural is having the wrong ticket for the only route available. Cyclical is when the whole transport system shuts down in a recession.
steps: Frictional: between jobs; structural: skills/location mismatch; cyclical: recession.
Recessionary gap on AD–AS: Y < Yf, cyclical unemployment rises.

flashcards.back: People of working age who are without work, available to start within two weeks, and actively seeking employment.
Frictional (between jobs), structural (skills/location mismatch), cyclical/demand-deficient (insufficient AD), seasonal (off-season industries).
Unemployment rate = (Number unemployed ÷ Labour force) × 100%, where labour force = employed + unemployed.
Claimant count: those receiving unemployment benefit (understates). ILO/LFS: internationally comparable survey based on seeking work (preferred measure).
The rate at full employment (Yf) — includes frictional and structural unemployment but not cyclical. Also called NAIRU.

flashcards.front: Policy for cyclical vs structural unemployment?
flashcards.back: Cyclical: expansionary fiscal/monetary to boost AD. Structural: education, retraining, relocation grants, supply-side reform.

sections.content: **Unemployment** (4.5) connects to **AD–AS** (4.3) and **macro policy** (5.x). Cambridge essays often ask you to **identify the type**, **calculate the rate**, and **evaluate** policy effectiveness.

Unemployment rate = $\frac{\text{Unemployed}}{\text{Labour force}} \times 100$

Labour force = Employed + Unemployed

Participation rate = $\frac{\text{Labour force}}{\text{Working-age population}} \times 100$

items: **Structural**: mismatch — wrong skills or location; may persist.
**Cyclical**: rises in recessions when AD is deficient.

sections.content:
**Demand-deficient (cyclical) unemployment** occurs when AD is too low — equilibrium Y falls **below Yf**, creating a **recessionary gap**. Firms cut output and lay off workers.

**Expansionary fiscal or monetary policy** shifts AD right, closing the gap and reducing cyclical unemployment.

sections.question:
In Country B:
- Working-age population: 10 million
- Employed: 5.4 million
- Unemployed: 0.6 million
- Economically inactive: 4.0 million

(a) Calculate the labour force and unemployment rate.
(b) The economy is in recession with Y = $450bn and Yf = $500bn. Explain the type of unemployment likely to dominate.
(c) Suggest one fiscal policy to reduce this unemployment and show on AD–AS.
sections.solution:
(a) **Labour force** = Employed + Unemployed = 5.4 + 0.6 = **6.0 million**

**Unemployment rate** = (0.6 ÷ 6.0) × 100 = **10%**

(b) **Type of unemployment**
Y ($450bn) < Yf ($500bn) → **negative output gap** of $50bn → **cyclical (demand-deficient) unemployment** dominates as firms reduce output.

(c) **Fiscal policy**
**Increase G** (e.g. infrastructure) or **cut taxes** → AD shifts **right** → Y rises toward Yf → firms rehire → cyclical unemployment falls.

On AD–AS: draw AD₁ left of full capacity; AD₂ after expansionary fiscal shift; new equilibrium closer to Yf.

sections.content: Always **name the unemployment type** before recommending policy. Expansionary demand policies fail against **structural** unemployment — training and supply-side measures are needed instead.

steps.caption: Unemployment types: frictional, structural, cyclical, seasonal.
steps.embedHint: Recessionary gap — Y below Yf on AD–AS diagram.

steps.caption: Cyclical (demand-deficient) unemployment rises in recessions.
steps.embedHint: Leftward AD shift — lower Y and downward pressure on P.

steps.caption: Full employment: unemployment at NAIRU/natural rate — not zero.
steps.embedHint: Mark Yf on LRAS — some frictional/structural remains.

steps.caption: Policies: fiscal/monetary to boost AD; supply-side for structural.
steps.embedHint: Predict policy shift direction to close recessionary gap.
